replies.author: @Bill-n9l7d
replies.text: ​​@ThinkingandTinkeringwell don't know how long I'll be connected so I like you a lot here is a tool I made that I use in my research I took a 2×4 inch copper aluminum and brass sheets leaving a stem on the end of each plate to connect wires to. Wrap the copper sheet with cling wrap like the ladies use in the kitchen. I sandwiched it between the other plates. The connection point for copper on one side of the module and the aluminum and brass connecting points on the other side. Wrap the assembly with electricians tape. And connect an antenna wire to the copper and the brass connections to the positive side of your meter. The aluminum to the negative. Now set your antenna up designed to capture ions or static charges. Then using a balloon rub up and down on a wooly fabric against your body it makes a better static charge. If you rub up and down you basically pick up nothing on the meter but if you rub one way is when you pick up reading after a static charge gets built up in the room. The greater the charge in the room the further away you can move from the antenna. Stop rubbing now and move next to the antenna. If you bump the balloon above the antenna you get next to nothing if anything but position the ball to the side of it and bump the balloon or should say jiggle. You get lots of ions. Doing it that way. So gravity does have an effect on ions of course. However they don't fall straight down 😊 normal a sensor like this has all three sheets of aluminum but it just detects the presence of ions. This sensor will weird them into a readable charge. It starts high and drops quickly of course I use a 60 dollar meter. I wonder what you will read on your lab meter 😊 I hope you like it. Maybe you haven't made one already if not you're going to have lots of fun. Making and using it. I think. Maybe I'll stay connected for a while it's hard to know cheers 😊

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
Hmm I'm thinking 2 magnet springs, of opposite polarity, over a rotating base, itself containing 2 magnets of opposite polarity. 
To "arm" the base plate put it into attraction position, lock/trigger gets set and then base can be rotated 180 degrees. I like the idea of using the relatively small amount of force of sliding the magnet out of their fields to allow the opposite magnetic field it to arm itself being quick and energy efficient.
Though of course a similar base design would work with a single magnet spring.

As magnetic force drops away with distance both attraction and repulsion do,  I started considering why not make the magnet tube double ended, the top being set up to attract to a magnet, fixed at the max height of travel. My thinking there is based on, as the bottom repulsion fades with the distance travelled away, the top attraction increases because it's nearer by the same amount, giving a more consistent torque throughout it's range. Which then led me to also start thinking about how to do a polarity swap and an easy arming mechanism.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @northwind7409
text: You can potentially increase the catapult range by using both compression and tension in your magnets. With compression only, you get good acceleration at the beginning of the stroke, but the force diminishes as the magnets get further apart. With tension only, the catapult arm would start out very slowly and only accelerate as it gets closer to the end magnet. By using a second magnet in the attraction arrangement at the other end, you would get closer to a steady power throughout the stroke. All good fun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's a great idea mate - I might give that a go!

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
I suspect that it might become too powerful, and more so at the end of the motion, where it is the least desirable, causing the arm to slam into the structure. 
The decreasing field strength of the repelling magnets behaves a lot like the decreasing torque of a twisted rope or bent stick, and tends to match the gravitational pull on the arm, which decreases exponentially as the arm rotates from vertical motion to horizontal. 
If anything, I'd put an extra adjustable repelling magnet by the end of the travel distance, to cause the arm to slow down and bounce back near its vertical angle so that the payload gets released while it's still moving upwards to some adjustable degree.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @tomlavedas44
text: I think there may actually be some potential for "wear and tear" in this approach, in that constantly changing the magnetic field of the magnets by having them oppose each other in close proximity could lead to a reorientation of the magnetic domains in the magnets and thereby reduce their field strength.  It is a common way of demagnetizing ferrous materials.  Admittedly it might be very minor, but misery tends to be conserved, especially in mechanical systems ;^).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are dead right mate - but it would take absolutely ages with these magnets and in this system - we are talking years - many many years lol

replies.author: @tomlavedas44
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Agreed. In a practical sense you are absolutely correct.  I was just trying to point out that there is no free lunch in a theoretical sense, at least.  Cheers.

author: @jimmyjohnson7241
text:
Another interesting video to get one thinking about how things work.  
A number of years ago I tried to make a more compact mechanical device to hurl a small projectile.   It gave me an  appreciation for the  complexity of the problem, and the genius of the bow and arrow mechanism. Here are a couple of additional comments on energy storage and energy transfer from a fixed source  to an object that is to be propelled at high velocity:

First, an efficient mechanical energy storage device for such a system  has to transfer the majority of its energy to the projectile.  At the end of its force stroke the kinetic energy in the parts of the mechanism should be as close to zero as possible.  This implies the the mechanism and all of its parts should be stopped,  not rotating, and in the fully relaxed state.  Very light weight for the energy stored makes the task much easier.

Second, the mechanism can best be understood as an impedance transformer between the energy in the storage to the kinetic energy of the projectile.  Because the mehanism should be stopped at the point that the projectile is at its maximum velocity ,  the transformation of velocity between the energy source and the projectile needs to be highly non uniform during the course of the transfer process, aproaching infinite velocity of output for minimal  velocity of the storage system components.

In the bow and arrow at the bow moves considerable at the fully drawn position in relation to the arrow movement, but has a near zero movement for arrow movement as the string approaches a straight line.  This highly variable ratio of storage mechanism movement to projectile movement  in a very light mechanical arrangement  is what makes the bow and arrow so efficient.  Pure genius!!
 
The other effective system using compressed air and explosives gas pressure is more of the combination of extremely light storage medium with enormous energy density.  It is more of a brute force system from an efficiency standpoint, but obviously highly effective.

The work on electromagnetic projectiles, like the railgun, open up the possibility of tailoring a very large energy storage system with no storage system mass through tailorable variable impedance force transfer to the projectile .   This is the reason for the billions of dollars that has been invested in developing such systems.

Jimmy Johnson

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for the detailed breakdown, Jimmy!

author: @Yezpahr
text:
I'm guessing the next video is about the ball magnet railgun.
It's a really cool toy, but can be quite dangerous. It's just a long gutter with several pairs of ball magnets in the gutter. To fire, you insert 1 ball magnet and roll it to the first 2 magnets.
The rolling ball magnet is pulled in, the middle one ejects the last one into the next 2 ball magnets with a speed that's amplified by the incoming magnet. So each pair along the way accelerates it
